front: Photograph: collaboration with James Agee documenting three Alabama sharecropper families pseudonymously called Gudger, Woods, and Ricketts (real names: Burroughs, Fields, Tingle)
back: Let Us Now Praise Famous Men (Walker Evans)
work: Let Us Now Praise Famous Men (1941)

front: Photograph: portrait of Allie Mae Burroughs, a thin-lipped Alabama sharecropper's wife, later appropriated by Sherrie Levine in After Walker Evans (1981)
back: Allie Mae Burroughs / Let Us Now Praise Famous Men (Walker Evans)
work: Let Us Now Praise Famous Men (1941)

front: Photograph: opening section 'On the Porch' is preceded by the lines 'The house had now descended / All over Alabama the lamps are out'
back: Let Us Now Praise Famous Men (Walker Evans)
work: Let Us Now Praise Famous Men (1941)

front: Photograph: over 600 candid portraits taken secretly on the NYC subway by hiding a 35mm Contax camera under his coat and triggering the shutter via a cable in his sleeve
Artist: famously declared 'Color photography is vulgar'
Artist: photographer Robert Frank (The Americans) was his student
Artist: befriended Ernest Hemingway while photographing for Carleton Beals's book The Crime of Cuba
